title.emphasis: The Legal Framework: Defining a Service Contract in California

section.para: In California, the determination of whether an act constitutes a service begins with the "Predominant Factor Test." This test is used to discern the "essence of the agreement" in hybrid transactions that involve both labor and the creation of a good, such as IP.1 When creation is externally directed, the value for the directing party lies not in the resulting digital file or artifact as a standalone "good," but in the specialized skill, creative effort, and intellectual labor invested by the creator to generate that IP.1 The predominant factor is overwhelmingly the user's service, which triggers the application of California's labor laws.1

Once this threshold is met, the relationship is scrutinized under the state's rigid, three-pronged "ABC test," which was established in Dynamex Operations West, Inc. v. Superior Court and applies retroactively.1 The test presumes a worker is an employee unless the hiring entity can prove all three of the following:

listitem.para: (A) The worker is free from the control and direction of the hiring entity.
(B) The worker performs work that is outside the usual course of the hiring entity's business.
(C) The worker is customarily engaged in an independently established trade, occupation, or business.1

section.para: The concept of "external direction" directly challenges Prong A. If an outside party is directing the work, the creator is, by definition, not entirely free from control. This legal framework looks past contractual labels to the functional reality of the relationship, making the act of direction a key determinant of a service relationship.1
